[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my forty-seventh published book and my second for Barnes & Noble's house publisher -- Sterling Press, as it was then called. (It's Union Square these days.) The Occult Book was still in process when the editors decided they wanted something similar about conspiracies. Since 2017 was a challenging year for me, with a difficult relocation and a range of other challenges to cope with, this was the only book I published in 2018, but it made up for that by selling like hotcakes. Like The Occult Book, it's a coffee table book of 100 historical vignettes from the history of secret societies and conspiracies, lavishly illustrated and with an ample bibliography. If you're interested, you can get a copy here if you live in the US and from your favorite local or online bookseller otherwise.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Afterlife stuff and similar
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I have been meditating on your book of occult philosophy and it has brought up some questions for me about the organization of why we have lives at all. I find it odd that we should all be part of the divine yet have to come to earth and lead such depressing lives full of the usual human nastiness. Then, I was reading Dune and at the back in the appendix was a quote from the planetary ecologist that said something like, "Existence is not a problem to be solved. It is a reality to be experienced." Is there some wisdom in this quote?

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Afterlife stuff and similar
replies.content:
There's a great deal of wisdom in that quote, as in many corners of that particular book.

From the perspective of occult philosophy -- I'll borrow a turn of phrase from Rosicrucian author George Winslow Plummer here -- the universe is the Divine thinking itself into manifestation. Our current incarnations, blissful, baleful, or boring as those might be, are part of the process of creation by which the Divine comes to know itself and express itself fully. Yes, some of that experience seems depressing at the moment! Each intelligent species, however, works out all its possibilities for good and evil during the course of its history, and all those possibilities become part of the self-knowledge of the Divine and also part of the individual self-knowledge of created beings such as you and me.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife stuff and similar
replies.content:
Thank you for that very thoughtful answer. I have another question about souls becoming temporarily trapped between the first an second deaths. Is this what happens in the case of suicide? In what other circumstances does this happen and are these souls what we experience as ghosts?
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Afterlife stuff and similar
replies.content: No, the situation with suicides is a delay after the second death. People get trapped between the first and second deaths most often via sudden, violent deaths when they still have etheric bodies at full strength -- that's why so many ghosts are the product of murders and other tragedies.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife stuff and similar
replies.content: Other than prayer, is there anything the living can do to help someone who died a sudden violent death to get 'untrapped'?

replies.title: Re: Afterlife stuff and similar
replies.content: Depends on how much magic you know. It's possible to free someone like that using advanced ceremonial magic, but that takes years of practice to do effectively.

comments.title: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Intrepid Fellow Students--
I am about 1/3 of the way through a very interesting book;
Laberge S, Rheingold H. Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming, A step by step guide. Ballantine Books, 1990. ISBN 978-0345420121

This is a guidebook that gives the reader exercises which increase the likelihood of having lucid dreams.
A lucid dream is a dream in which you are aware that you are having a dream, but you are able to stay in the dream and exercise some control over what happens in the dream.

By the way it is written, the authors seem to have some familiarity with occult studies and disciplines. Its likely that any Magic Monday reader will find the instructions a bit on the elementary side, but will also have no problem doing the exercises very well! Its early days for me, but I have already had some minor success with lucid dreaming in less than a week.

JMG and other readers, does anyone regularly have lucid dreams? If so, have they been useful in your occult studies, specifically in interacting with archetypes?

I think I remember that Dion Fortune described, in one of her writings, that people had been able to intentionally meet on the astral plane and that their separate accounts of such meetings matched when they compared notes after awakening. This was also discussed in My Big Toe by Thomas Campbell (pg 85), also I've heard that Australian Aboriginals know how to do this.
Has anyone had experience with meeting other people on the astral plane that they know in physical life?

I wonder, JMG, if you could make more sense of your more incomprehensible dreams if you were able to become lucid and ask the other players what is going on?
Thanks for doing Magic Mondays, and thanks in advance for any comments or information!

-Polkadot Shabby Grouse

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content:
One of the core techniques in the dreamwork manual I've been using, The Jungian-Senoi Dreamwork Manual by Strephon Kaplan Williams, is a sort of retrospective dream lucidity; you can enter a dream in active imagination and dialogue with the figures in it. Since I've been doing active imagination pretty regularly for the last forty years, this works well for me. Of course there's also meditating on the images...

With regard to astral meetings, yes, that's quite a common practice in some Western occult groups. It's usually done in active imagination rather than in dreams, but I know of no reason why lucid dreamers couldn't do the same thing.

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content:
Hi Shabby Grouse,

I do become aware that I am dreaming in some dreams, though I don't practice "lucid dreaming" as such, and I don't ever try to force it. Many of my most powerful and meaningful dreams I was fully immersed in and never really knew that it was a dream until something woke me up. I'm all for dream exploration, journaling, and practice, but I think people get hung up on lucid dreaming. It's kind of similar to the concept of "astral tourism."

As to your other question: I have had experiences where I am with certain people in dreams, and I think it is on some level them, but since it is not a lucid dream or strict astral projection as such, its hard to know for sure, but you get a sense of it as you go.

I think the best thing you can do to gain more dream awareness is just the basic practice of dream journaling. This will in itself lead you to recall more of your dreams and by extension, you will probably become lucid -especially if you allow yourself to sleep in- and it will be spontaneous rather than forced.

More interesting to me is the deliberate cultivation of hypnagogia rather than lucid dreaming. Hypnagogic naps can be incredibly strange and creative.

See:

https://www.artofmanliness.com/health-fitness/health/hypnagogic-nap/

Interesting article here about Charles Dickens and his naps and dreams:

https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.700882/full

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content:
Hi PSG,
I've always been a very active dreamer (can normally recall 2-4 dreams a night.) In high school, I saw the movie Waking Life which is about dreaming and lucid dreaming in particular. That sent me down a rabbit hole and I started dream journaling and trying some practices. One was to ask myself throughout the day if I was dreaming, and then, after it became an ingrained habit, I would do that in dreams and eventually discover I WAS actually dreaming. It worked and along with other practices, I had a period of lucid dreaming with some regularity, though I didn't quite reach the ability to do whatever I wanted, still being constrained by some dream rules and logic.

Though I was prone to nightmares, I also had lots of lovely dreams and also often had dreams that ended up resonating out into my life in interesting ways. I found that my work with lucid dreaming wasn't altogether rewarding and actually somewhat diminished my enjoyment of my dream life. For me, I feel receptive in dreams and often wake with new connections in mind and enjoy sharing the dreams with other close friends. I'm controlling so much in life, that dreamtime (even when it's active and not particularly restful) is a different experience that I value.

Something that I've kept though is the ability to strengthen my dream self with my daily habits; and occult study has been a big part of that. My questions this month actually include discussion of some of that. The short is that the practices I do daily are more readily available to me in dreams.

Wishing you well,
Hearthculture

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content: Does anyone have any tricks for waking up from a lucid dream? My daughter once got stuck in one, she said she was walking around her school for about half an hour trying to figure out how to wake up. She only woke up because I shook her awake for sleeping in. Strange problem I know!

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content: I used to have this kind of lucid dreams fairly often, first time this happened I even had a panic attack inside a dream, thinking, after trying to wake up for what felt like hours, I must have died in my sleep, and now am experiencing disintegration of my psyche. What worked for me is trying to fall asleep within a dream, to wake up in reality. After I've started practicing practicing daily banishing ritual those dreams went away, so this might be an option too.

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content:
Falling, jump off something (not too high or intimidating - don't want to implant that any part of your conscious) and the sensation of falling can wake you.

There is a similar technique for the reverse, if you have trouble because you want to lucid dream but are waking up the moment you realize you are dreaming, you can fall back to your butt or do something else jarring to your dream body to help stay in it.

For me the difference seems to be, the falling (swoop in the belly) feeling wakes, the gentle bump on the dream body helps you stay asleep.

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content: Thank you to both the replies, I will pass that on to her so she has some things to try if it happens again.

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content: Look at your hands. That does it.

replies.title: Re: Lucid Dreaming--Book Report
replies.content:
I have had a few lucid dreams, but they were unforced and unexpected. They usually don't last long; I seldom wake up less than a minute after becoming lucid.

I have also had dreams where I recognize that I am dreaming but the realization of what that implies doesn't seem to kick in. My subconscious just goes "Oh, I'm dreaming" in the same nonchalant way I might think "Oh, I'm posting on Ecosophia" and gives it no more mind.

I have also had cases where this happens in reverse: I reach a level of conscious awareness where I should go lucid but don't; I consciously cogitate and reason, but I do so as my dream character and according to the context of the dream. (This likely usually happens when nothing too unreal is occurring.)

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology
replies.content: Astrology has a long and dignified history in the Muslim world and has generally been considered theologically acceptable in Islam. That seems to be guiding a lot of people in the Resistance these days. I noted, in a chapter in my forthcoming book on mundane astrology, that the date and time of the formal proclamation of the Islamic Republic of Afghanistan was obviously chosen for its astrological value (and in fact the astrologer in question did a first-rate job). So I'd expect to see more of this sort of thing as we proceed.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology
replies.content:
Some years back Deb Houlding did a presentation at an AFA conference analyzing the foundation chart for the city of Baghdad in 762 CE. It was an amazing chart (and Deb is an amazing astrologer), and it was obviously elected for a long-lasting dynasty, and was tied in with the planned shape of Baghdad built on astrological lines.

The presentation was shortly after the capture and execution of Saddam Hussein, and Houlding showed correlations with the original chart and that current event.

So yes, astrology is alive and well in the Arab world and has been for millennia as you likely know, and much of our best source material for Hellenistic astrology was kept alive and transmitted in the Arab world.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,
For the last few years I have been running a modestly successful blog (an average of 10-12,000 views a week for current and previous articles.) I hadn't originally thought of asking for money, but as time went on people began to ask about payment and buying me coffees, so I allowed this. I acquired a small number of paying subscribers, which increased gradually, but recently the number has stagnated and even declined slightly. I found this hard to understand, but after much meditation I realised two things. One was that I'd spent my professional life, including in academia, writing as part of my job. Expecting to actually get paid just for writing seemed by comparison faintly indecent. The other is that, for reasons I well understand, I've been chronically incapable of asking for things, or accepting them when offered all my life. I'm not sure that further meditation or journaling will actually shed much more light on the question. The question is what I can do to release blockages and allow whatever people want to give me to come my way. (I've used the Emotional Freedom Technique successfully over the years.)

I tried charity donations to the Sun (to an eye hospital) and it's true that over the last few weeks there's been a small but noticeable increase in page views and engagement, and a handful of new paid subscribers. But is there anything I can do that will attract whatever level of financial reward my writing genuinely deserves? I'm beginning to wish I hadn't agreed to accept payments in the first place! As both a professional writer and magician , I thought you might have some ideas.
Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, the first thing you need to do is unpack the feelings of inadequacy and self-hatred that have you thinking that you don't deserve to receive a fair payment for your hard work. The academic industry has helped to mire you in those emotional bad habits, so that it can profit at your expense -- look at the absurd prices that academic publishers and journals charge these days, and think about the fact that none of the authors who actually write the books and articles are getting a cent in return -- but it may not be just that. My suggestion? You need to grapple with the attitudes that have you convinced that, unlike any other craftsperson, you shouldn't be paid for your labor. Magic won't do it; you have to change your thinking.

Second, be aware that the economies of the developed world are all in deep trouble right now. Every writer I know has less income than he or she did a few years ago, because so many people are struggling to make ends meet and can't afford to pay writers. That is to say, it's not just you. You may need to reflect on what you write, and do some things that more people find valuable enough to pay for, if you want more money.

replies.title: Re: Mark Stavish et al. - The Body of Light
replies.content: Stavish's system, if I understand it correctly, is intended to create an astral construct that will survive physical death. It exists on the astral plane and keeps the astral body from being dissolved after death. replies.title: Re: Mark Stavish et al. - The Body of Light
replies.content:
Dear JMG,


[Not the OP]


I was going to ask you about Stavish's views on the Body of Light, but the OP beat me to it!


Stavish is of the opinion that the formation of the Body of Light should be central to all esoteric practices. He has stated this on several podcasts and in his books.


Why do you not recommend it? Should we just let nature take its course after we die?

replies.title: Re: Mark Stavish et al. - The Body of Light
replies.content:
First of all, any time anybody says "x should be central to all esoteric practices," they're smoking their shorts. (I say this despite the fact that Mark's a friend of mine and I have a profound respect for most of his work.) One size emphaticaly does not fit all. In fact, since no two human souls are identical -- we each have our own karma and our own destiny, and both of these differ from those of every other soul in the entire cosmos -- advice that is good for one person is often hideously wrong for many others.

Second, the cosmos knows what it's doing. We do not. How many times do we have to go blundering around, serenely convinced in our utter ignorance that we know how to fix the cosmos, before the inevitable disasters finally teach us that maybe this isn't as good an idea as we like to think? Death is not that big a deal; we've all been through it before, and some of us remember the process. Nothing is ever really lost; just as the lives the soul of Mozart put into music before his most famous birth had him playing the violin on the concert stage at the age of six, and writing some of the best music our species has ever created, the talents you develop and the initiatory connections you establish stay with you from life to life as you work your way through the human level and finish the job that material incarnation is there to do. I suggest that, instead of frantically trying to piece together an escape capsule in the hope of dodging nature, it's a much better idea to trust the process, and focus on clearing karma, developing skills and talents, and pursuing our connections with the spiritual realm and the Divine, so that we can finish up our work on the material plane, pass through death calmly, and land on the other side in good condition to take up our work there.

comments.title: What is Love? Part 2
comments.content:
Last week in response to my question about a new romantic relationship, JMG wrote:

'You have been programmed by the media to fixate on certain fashionable kinds of female appearance and to think that this matters more than, say, compatibility or character... I'm going to suggest that you do some serious journaling, and explore the way that you've been set up to sabotage real relationships with women and chase a mirage of appearance.'

I want to thank everyone who responded last week, and to share some of the results of my journaling here, in case anyone was interested or could benefit from what I've learned. I'll put in a link to last week's full thread at the bottom of this post.

If we understand the media as a kind of crass mass-market thaumaturgy, then yes, media has a magical effect on men's perceptions of women, and true theurgy cannot avoid working to undo that effect. Part of it is the nature of visual media: everything important in a movie has to be communicated visually, so the most effective way to demonstrate the hero's inner worth as a man is by having the most visually attractive woman on-screen choose him as her mate.

Therein lies the rub. We men chase beautiful women partly because we want them (sometimes desperately) but I think mostly, and more subtly, to validate our sense of self-worth. That's why 'trophy wives' are a thing. If you can get with the hottest women, it must be because you're really worth something. Women deal with this internal dynamic too, but it plays out differently for them.

So when two good-looking people get together, on that basis alone, they're often entering an unspoken deal that goes something like 'You prop up my sense of self-worth and I'll prop up yours, and the orgasms will be the icing on the cake'. As a form of social game this at least makes sense and is mutually beneficial. The trouble is that it has nothing to do with, say, getting along, or working together on shared life goals, or even with having good sex.

Steiner says somewhere that immediately after death is a long period in which the spirit slowly sheds its astral body, enduring a state of still having those astral habits and desires acquired in life, but without a body with which to satisfy those desires. This is apparently where the idea of purgatory comes from. I feel that I'm going through something like that these days, as I walk in the park and see all the pretty women I'm never going to get with because of my chosen commitment to my lady love. My days of porn and swimsuit models are far in the past (thank gods) but I suspect I am now facing a process of undoing their magical influence, while still incarnated, and it hurts.

(Link to last week's thread here: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/298041.html?thread=51349305#cmt51349305)

replies.title: Re: What is Love? Part 2
replies.content: Good. If you get that over now, you won't have to slog through it after death. In the meantime, I have another exercise for you to practice. Look at your lady and repeat silently to yourself the mantra, "Beautiful." Do this 100 times a day. Yes, you can do that all at once -- say she's sitting on the couch reading something, you can consider her and repeat the mantra 100 times right then -- or you can break up the repetitions. Media sorcery works by repetition of simple concepts, and it can be broken the same way.

She doesn't even have to know- she'll just get to experience me looking into her eyes when we're reunited and hearing me say 'Beautiful' and mean it from the bottom of my heart. I'd say that's worth working through purgatory for.

comments.title: Fiction
comments.content:
I've recently been getting back into reading more works of fiction, and I was wondering if you could come up with a rough top-10 list of novels, series, and/or authors that you have enjoyed the most and that have had a strong influence on the development of your thoughts and ideas.

Any recommendations would be greatly appreciated! Thank you very much.

replies.title: Re: Fiction
replies.content:
That's a tough one, because fiction has been one of my great pleasures since earliest childhood, and selecting ten or so books, series, or authors from the many thousands that I've read and enjoyed will not be easy. It's tougher now because some of the books that influenced me powerfully in my younger days are not things I'd recommend now -- Tolkien, for example, brilliant as he is, has fostered a disastrous cult of black-and-white political moralizing that is making a steaming mess of our world. Still, I'll give it a try. Here are ten authors to consider.

- Hermann Hesse. He was as popular as Tolkien in the 1960s and 1970s, but he's been erased since then. I've summarized his brilliant novels here; start with Demian.

- John Crowley. His early SF and fantasy novels, from The Deep through Little, Big, are utterly brilliant; later on, he got lionized by the New York critical establishment and sucked into writing Literary Fiction, and it spoiled him as a writer. Start with Beasts, one of the classic works of deindustrial fiction.

- Edgar Pangborn. As far as I know he's been completely forgotten, but his Davy is another classic deindustrial novel, a rollicking, funny, tragic, intensely human novel set some centuries from now in a post-global warming, post-nuclear war neomedieval American Atlantic coast. Half the postapocalyptic fiction after his time cribbed shamelessly from Davy. His other novels and short stories are also worth a read, but start with Davy.

- Dion Fortune. Her real name was Violet Firth Evans and she mostly wrote occult nonfiction, but her four great occult novels -- The Goat-Foot God, The Winged Bull, The Sea Priestess, and Moon Magic -- are well worth reading. You might also pick up The Keys to the Temple by Penny Billington and Ian Rees, which explains what's going on in those four books for those who aren't up to speed on her end of occultism. Start with The Goat-Foot God.

- Robert Shea and Robert Anton Wilson. Both of them had independent careers on their own, but like McCartney and Lennon, they needed each other to achieve greatness and dropped to second-rate status once they parted ways. The Illuminatus! trilogy is their one great work. It's kind of a 1970s period piece these days, but it's still a wild, rollicking, funny and magical work, worth your while.

- Rosemary Sutcliff. She's the odd one out here, mostly an author of children's books -- but what children's books! Bound to a wheelchair for almost all her life due to a rare illness, she made British history her field and wrote dozens of amazing novels set in every period from the Stone Age to the Napoleonic wars, most for children and some for adults. Even her kids' books are readable by adults -- but if you have kids, get them some Sutcliff. They'll thank you. I'd encourage you to start with Sword at Sunset, one of the best Arthur-as-he-really-was novels ever penned, or The Mark of the Horse Lord, a tale of Roman Britain and the Scottish and Pictish border tribes.

- Eric Ambler. He's the guy who realized that spy thrillers pitting invulnerable heroes against cackling villains were duller than ditchwater, and started writing subtle, wry, intelligent novels about ordinary people caught up in the realities of international espionage and political intrigue. Start with A Coffin for Dimitrios, the best of his prewar novels; whatever else of his you do or don't read, don't miss A Passage of Arms or Doctor Frigo, which will teach you more about modern international politics than any fifty nonfiction books on the subject, and make it fun.

- Raymond Chandler. Dashiell Hammett invented the noir mystery but Chandler brought it to a peak that has been endlessly imitated but never equaled, much less surpassed. He deploys an enormous amount of literary skill under a hard-edged facade. Start with The Long Sleep.

- Dorothy Sayers. Most of the classic English mystery writers of the early 20th century wrote ingenious puzzles in which characters less well rounded than your average cardboard cutout go through arbitrary gyrations at the author's command. Then there was Dorothy Sayers, who wrote first-rate novels that happen to center on mystery plots. Start with Whose Body?, the first of her Lord Peter Wimsey mysteries, and read them in order; the best are the last three.

I also love the Peter Wimsey stories I've read; I'll have to re-vist the series and finish it to the end.  Really looking forward to checking out the other authors.  Much obliged!

A data point as we approach November…

I have been reading a small, niche blog on a (non-political) hobby topic for 20+ years, and stuck with it as the blogger has gone from an intelligent, thoughtful, mostly apolitical person to someone literally radicalized by NPR, who works at a library and identifies as a feminist and fantasizes about retribution against “right wingers” (evil people she doesn’t agree with). I now check her latest posts every few weeks just to get my finger on the pulse of the ideal Harris voter, which she is. (Though she was a diehard Warren fan in 2020.)

Well. She ordered some official campaign merchandise from Harris/Walz almost 4 weeks ago, and it still hasn’t shipped. T-shirts, bracelets, stickers, all of it. Commenters on her blog report the same thing. None of their stuff has shipped yet either, and they are getting ignored or rebuffed by customer service, and are starting to get uneasy.

Separately, this blogger noticed a lot of Trump signs in her neighborhood, and went directly to a local Democrat headquarters to get a Harris sign, but they had none and blew her off. Others in the comments had similar troubles getting an official sign. The blogger resorted to ordering a knock-off Made in China one from Amazon.

I know this seems like an “all material world” data point, but it dovetails with the many odd metaphysical events and signatures of this whole election cycle, and the emptiness and scamminess of the what it signifies is notable to me.

Also, did you see Harris campaigning with the Cheney family (who endorsed her) under the slogan “Country Over Party”? Absolutely astonishingly dumb. It sounds like “A party to celebrate the country being over.” It’s up there with “Stop Asian Hate.” And when “Country Above Party” is right there! Yet they didn’t use it. It’s a basic advertising fail, not even advanced magical technique. Are they under some kind of self-defeat spell? Or is it deliberate—they have to be honest to some degree? Granted, I am not sad about it. Just fascinated.

comments.title: Dream banishings and gated fellowships
comments.content:
Good afternoon JMG and commentariat,

I had a strange experience one night a couple weeks ago. In the years since I started down the path of occult study and magic practice, I have on occasion revisited nightmares from my youth, or been in unsettling new dreams that I could tell were linked to a past nightmare. As I progressed with training, daily banishing, and meditation, during those scary dream moments, I've often been able to actively work in the dream to either banish or heal the entity or imbalance sometimes with a great sense of relief or joy afterward.

In the dream a couple weeks ago, I was in an attic and recognized a dark corner linking it to a nightmare in youth. I felt resolve and approached it and saw a strange light that felt angry and seemed like it was send fear at me, I told it it was not welcome and had to leave, and then it started flashing horrific images before my eyes. I started drawing the SOP symbols asking the elements to help me banish it, and I was suddenly on the opposite side of the attic stunned as if nothing had happened. I saw a glimmer and ran back over to the corner where the entity flashed and hurled the fear and images at me, and then I started doing the CGD LBRP, and made it part way through and it seemed to diminishing and then I woke. I felt ok upon waking leading me to think it worked.

(I'm open to thoughts from the commentariat on all questions)

1. Have you heard of people doing this in dreams, and/or do you have any opinions or thoughts on the matter?

2. Since I started successfully confronting beings in my dreams I've rarely been properly afraid in a dream (whereas I used to be plagued by nightmares,) but this one shook me. My primary work is with the SOP, this was the first time I've used it out in a dream however, and it had an effect but for some reason the LBRP felt like a stronger tool in that moment. I seem to recall you mentioning that the LBRP is perhaps stronger in banishing (or maybe that was just in reference to the Heathen LBRP being "like a slamming door" rather than a clearing. I believe you've also remarked in the past about Golden Dawn magic being a multi-tool. Do you have any thoughts or speculations on this element of the dream, the difference in banishings, and/or the seeming strength of the LBRP in this context?

3. I've been thinking about the SOP and its related traditions, and working on a tentative categorization placing the traditions into gates - with the intent to create a flowchart of sorts for how the traditions relate and what they resonate with. I'm curious to know your (and anyone else's) opinion on the following:

East Gate of Air: Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose (thinking of it as East facing, with a focus on divination and Guardian Angel work - and feels more "thinky" to me.)

South Gate of Fire: Golden Section Fellowship (thinking of the Eternal Spiritual Sun and the solar plexus will strengthening.)

West Gate of Water: Dolmen Arch (thinking of its focus on historical reading, deeply diving into myth, and reflecting upon it - the Dolmen Arch also just feels tree-ish to me when I think about it.)

North Gate of Earth: Ancient Order of Druids in America (thinking of the Earth and locale focused study and activities, and wildcraftfing of personal druidry.)

Below Gate of Spirit: Order of Spiritual Alchemy (thinking of it as internally focused and intended to clear up/ free up the spirit within through working on your own shadow to find the root issues.)

Above Gate of Spirit: Modern Order of Essenes (thinking of the intent on being a channel for spirit energies and the healing energy as being like starlight bridging vast distances.)

Within Gate of Spirit: Universal Gnostic Church and Gnostic Celtic Church (thinking of their emphasis on personal gnosis, personal practice, and mystical relationship to your understanding of the divine.)

As always, thank you for this gathering and for your counsel.

replies.title: Re: Dream banishings and gated fellowships
replies.content:
For what it's worth, and with an appropriate amount of salt, I had this non-dream experience when I was first starting out with magic (like, rank beginner, first few weeks, not counting a spell doing meditation on and divination with the Runes without any ritual component a year or two prior):

I was going to bed and turned all the lights off in the common area. Between there and the stairs up to our room is a door into a guest room which was billed as a "mother-in-law suite" (I've since learned that only really applies if it has its own outdoor access, which this does not, but it does have its own bathroom). As I walked by that door, I got a distinctly creepy feeling, and I looked hard at where it felt like it was coming from, but couldn't see or hear anything. Then, as I was looking, one of my cats walked up and sat down and stared right at the same spot. I made a sign of the hammer and said a quick prayer and headed upstairs to go to bed. Well, I kept feeling the creepy feeling coming from the same direction, so as I laid in bed, I did a mental Heathen LBRP (what I was practicing at the time), which seemed to make things okay and I was able to sleep.

The next day, I went down to the room to investigate in the light of day, which we had set up as a guest room at the time. I walked around and tried to get a feel, and again, very creepy, but not as strong as in the dark. So, I decided I'd pull out my brand new hammer, having found this obvious nail, and I tried to do a for-real LBRP in the room. Not only did I not feel like things were better, I got the distinct sense things were somehow worse (nothing dangerous or the like, at least not immediately, just it went from "kinda creepy" to "something is wrong"). So, letting better sense prevail, I sought back up and prayed about what to do, and a few things clicked into place.

First off, I remembered that our realtor had mentioned off-hand that the previous owners were motivated to sell because they had some kind of illness in the family. Secondly, the closet in that room had a weird feature - one of the bookshelves in it was on hinges and opened into a tiny, unfinished room. We always figured it was some weird artifact of an amateur addition or remodeling, but not long before all this, a neighbor had revealed that it had actually been a grow room for marijuana - hence the small footprint and hidden entrance. Putting these together with some insights from my prayer, I got the distinct impression that what I was feeling was something left over from a terminally ill lady who had lived in that room, and that the "creepiness" was more grief, fear, and confusion than malevolence, and that the better response was compassion.

So, I set up an altar cloth on a side table in the room, lit three candles for three Goddesses with whom I had a relationship and whom I thought could help, offered each a drink offering, lit some incense, and prayed to them asking to help heal and soothe whoever or whatever was there so she or it could move on, while sharing that at least somebody remembered her/it and was sorry for what had happened. After, I circled the room with the incense (some mugwort), and asked her/it to please leave.

I never felt the creepy feeling in there again, and in fact, it became my office when we had to rearrange things for the birth of our second daughter. Of course, it's possible that all of this was some combination of me wanting something "really magical" to happen as I opened my world to the unseen, projecting my feelings about my own mom's death a few years before, or just plain "all in my head," so who knows.

But the cat never paid attention to that spot again, either.

Best,
Jeff

comments.title: Trees, part VI; Occult history part IV
comments.content:
In a recent meeting with the great pine tree I've mentioned before, I felt something new, which I believe is etheric energy. I don't mean the "green highs" I've gotten before from our positive relations, this time I felt it in the tree itself -- a sort of undulating, powerful, living energy. Can you confirm that this is, in fact, the etheric sheath of the tree, or something else? Do large trees usually have such a strong aura? I also wonder why I'm suddenly able to sense this... all I can guess is that I'm doing something right.

Last week, I asked about the Tower of Babel, and got a rather fascinating answer (that it's a dim recollection of an ancient spacefaring power, early in the Atlantean cycle, and that the Confusion of Tongues recalls their lingua franca fracturing apart). This is on top of the Great Flood being the Drowning of Atlantis, and the Fall of Man as an echo of the Lemurian Deviation. So hopefully you don't mind me digging a bit more into the Old Testament, which as the collective record of an ancient culture seems to contain a lode of occult history, garbled as it may be.

So, one of the other famous incidents in the Old Testament is in regard to the Antediluvian world, when angels slept with human women, producing hybrids known as Nephilim, described as giants and mighty warriors (which I believe is one of the things that led to YHWH getting fed up and hitting a watery reset button). Anyway, what in the world was going on there? This seems to be another dim recollection of something much more ancient, but I can only guess.
I wonder if this has anything to do with the genetic experiments of Atlantis in its darker days, which produced hybrids like mermaids, sirens, centaurs, etc of Greek myth. Or maybe I'm getting off-track.

Anyway, thanks in advance! (and the reason this is the 4th occult history query is because the first two were focused on astrological ages)

comments.title: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
comments.content:
Hello John Michael Greer,

I find anything to do with a comparison of the SoP and LBRP, in fact anything that compares Druid Magic and Golden Dawn, fascinating. Earlier a thread mentioned the differences between each, where I read with great interest that "[the SoP is …] is better suited to students who don't intend to get into ceremonial magic as such [... then the LBRP]"

1) Is it possible to hear about the differences between Druid Magic as described in the DMH and Golden Dawn Magic, in terms of what can be accomplished and what are the objectives?

Last week I wrote a bit about using Druid Magic to invoke planets. I am also looking at traditional Golden Dawn, which a long time ago I worked with a bit, and Celtic Golden dawn with a lot of admiration for the RC ritual and the LVX formula. 2) Are the RC ritual and LVX formula adaptable to the DMH system?


3) Do the DMH Grail Cauldron, Dragon and Tree rituals aim for similar objectives?

Thank you,
JML

replies.title: Re: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
replies.content:
1) The DMH and Dolmen Arch systems are magical practices meant to work with the Sphere of Protection and the rest of John Gilbert's system. I consider the magic they teach to be subsidiary to the main meditative work of John's teachings: basically, enough magic that trained practitioners can chase off harmful energies, do certain kinds of healing and blessing work, and change anything dysfunctional about their lives. The GD, Celtic and otherwise, by contrast is a system that puts magic at the center of the spiritual path, and all the other methods of work -- meditation, clairvoyance, divination, etc. -- are there to make the magic more powerful. So if you want to meditate and do a little magic, you want the DMH/DA approach; if you want to practice magic and do a little meditation, you want the GD.

2) No, not at all. They're specific to the GD, and to the Hermetic GD at that.

3) No, they're closer in effect to the Middle Pillar exercise.

replies.title: Re: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
replies.content:
Hi,

*Not the OP*


Do the DMH practices like the Grail Cauldron, Dragon, Tree exercises have any similarity to qigong/neigong? What are the exercises designed to do?


Many thanks for Magic Monday.

replies.title: Re: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
replies.content:
Not to qigong, no -- as the name indicates, qigong is meant to work with etheric energy, the life force that circulates with the breath. Some forms of neigong, however, work with energies on the astral and higher level, and these are comparable to the DMH practices.

The purpose of the DMH exercises is to awaken certain energy centers in the astral body for improved health and spiritual development; the exercises have secondary effects on the etheric body, because qi (the life force) follows yi (conscious intention).

replies.title: Re: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
replies.content: Not the OP, but are there any hidden factors that prevent a student of the Golden Dawn from taking a more meditative approach to their system? Or is it a time management issue, the Sphere of Protection offering a single versatile ritual for a student to master, freeing time for other pursuits, in contrast with the Golden Dawn's multitude of rituals?

replies.title: Re: Differences between Druid Magic and Golden Dawn
replies.content: No hidden factors at all. In fact, the GD as originally practiced was more focused on meditation and occult study than it has since become -- it's simply that (a) the GD papers tacitly assume that you learned discursive meditation from your Anglican pastor if not your parents (it was normally taught to children in the Anglican church until around the time of the First World War), so no instruction was included, and (b) most of the work that went into developing the system later on went into the ceremonial magic side of it.

comments.title: Long range problems need addressing
comments.content:
Due to long and severe hardship that we have shared, my sister has become afflicted on multiple levels. First she has a physical problem that needs proper medical attention. I think it’s probably not too bad - she has seen highly qualified doctors who said as much, but that she needs to see a specialist - or better, a really great alternative practitioner.

But far worse is the psychological effect on her that appears to be associated with this condition. She has gone from a person who is basically happy by disposition to someone unwholesomely obsessed with a sense of ritual uncleanness, to a degree that looks like mental illness. It’s extraordinarily distressing to me to witness this.

I’ve done some simple tarot draws on this, and the most recent and far the clearest are these:

Q: What will best address the underlying causes of her mental affliction?

A: Nine of Cups

Q: What is the nature of her physical affliction, including its spiritual component?

A: Ace of Cups

I take this to mean that in the first case she needs material stability - something that has long been woefully lacking - to begin to restore her capacity for happiness. And in the second case, that her affliction at multiple levels has to do with her relationship to the element of water.

All this being the case, I would appreciate any constructive suggestions as to how I might begin to address the matter.

I say “begin” because I don’t think this is something that will be resolved in a jiffy, or in seven easy lessons. It looks like this is probably going to be a journey of some duration.

I imagine I should probably begin with proper attention to my own mental attitude. Presently I am bearing responsibilities that feel to me like the weight of the world, and this is one large burden too many. I need to decompress from it somehow, but circs do not make this easy.

replies.title: Re: Long range problems need addressing
replies.content: Do you have her consent to do any of this?

replies.title: Re: Long range problems need addressing
replies.content:
Yes. She has given permission for magic before, and has done so explicitly in this case. In fact she likes the idea.

This isn’t only for her. It’s also for me. Her company now makes Moaning Myrtle look like a delightful fun-loving companion. I am already carrying very heavy burdens. This is like the kitchen sink that broke the camel’s back. I can’t take it.

When I approach this on the material level, she is close to being unhelpable. I would suspect a touch of the Rescue Game, but if there’s a trace of that it’s not conscious. There is no one less manipulative or conniving.

replies.title: Re: Long range problems need addressing
replies.content: Thank you. Both of you need to seek professional help at once. You, because you're obviously under a great deal of stress, and her, because her problems are beyond what an amateur practitioner can handle. Set aside the tarot cards, pick up the phone, get her a referral to a specialist and also to a therapist, and then get yourself an appointment with somebody who can counsel you on how to deal with this very difficult situation.

comments.title: The Symbol of Freemasonry
comments.content: Hi JMG, keeping with the book covered in this weeks MM post, I wanted to ask your thoughts about the occult dimensions of the compass and ruler used by Freemasons. I have been drawn to it for years and find it compelling, almost hypnotic. I understand the why as spelled out in The Lost Keys of Freemasonry but there's something about it that is, well, no better way to say it than mystic, a mystery.

replies.title: Re: The Symbol of Freemasonry
replies.content: There's a lot there, but -- ahem -- since I'm a Mason myself, there's not all that much I can say about it. If you're eligible to join -- that is to say, male and a believer in a supreme being -- you might consider finding out the traditional way, by applying for membership.

replies.title: Re: The Symbol of Freemasonry
replies.content: Got it. I always come back to Groucho Marx, I never wanted to be part of a club that would have me as member. If memory serves me correctly, Groucho himself, was a Mason, so there's that. Is it common for men to apply later in life?

replies.title: Re: The Symbol of Freemasonry
replies.content: A good many new members -- up to half in some jurisdictions -- join when they reach retirement age. I'm not sure about Groucho, but Harpo was certainly a Mason.

comments.title: Paedrig's Pentacles
comments.content:
Hi there, Paedrig again. So my luck and fortune have been vastly improved by my pentacle of Jupiter - my ex and I are actually talking and might be amicably selling our shared property. Not what I was expecting or asking for - far better!

So I am starting to get into metalworking, even as I am getting more and more into talismanic magic. This week, I intend to finally be able to pour metals melted in a crucible - and I have a coin cast onto which I can carve anything that isnt too ornate.

So my friends have a wedding this weekend. I still need to get them a gift. I was thinking - a handmade decorative coin! I was going to put a symbol for each of them on each sides to symbolize the two of them merging theor lives into one. Something in bronze, since bronze lasts forever, to represent their marriage lasting forever. And finally, if they ever have an argument they cant resolve, they can settle it by flipping this coin! It seemed like a funny idea.

I began contemplating what symbol I should put on each side. The man is Jewish, and a fan of star trek and scooby doo. The woman is taiwanese, a fan of Harley Quinn from batman, harry potter, and considers herself a slytherim and a bit of a spooky witch, though she doesnt actually take the occult seriously.

Eventually, I realized the easiest thing to do is to do two simple symbols on each side - a hexagram on one side for the man, a pentagram on the other for the woman - with a few added bits. For instance, the star trek logo in the middle of the hexagram, and a snake eating is own tale in a circle around the pentagram. Personal touches.

However, it then occured to me that the hexagram is the second pentacle of Jupiter, whose metal is tin, while the pentagram makes the second pentacle of venus, whose metal is copper. And since bronze is copper combined with tin... could I perhaps make a talisman to both these planets? Make the talisman on thursday going into friday? Invoke bith energies - Jupiter for wealth and good fortune, venuse for wealth and domestic harmony?

Finally, is it bad to modify the pentacles? For instance, by adding the personal touches I mentioned, or even by skipping all the hebrew letters? I dont know if I can carve all of them - does it have to be a perfect reproduction to work? Or could just having the overall same shape and intent work?

Is any of this a good idea? Or are you not supposed to mix planetary energies with alloy talismans? Let me know what your advice is.

Sorry for posting so late, I work nights. Haunting the hours of darkness, I do my rituals my moonlight and starlight - or by the light of the planets. Jupiter is out at 3am when I get home. Its why I know I could craft the talisman late thursday going into friday. Anyway, thanks for your advice as always.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: Um, I understand that you work nights, but I'll be closing this in two minutes, so I'm not up to writing a lengthy essay on the theory of talismans. I recommend that you make the coin just a coin, and leave further talisman workings for situations when you have ample time to do the research and ask any questions you may have one or two at a time.
